original_hypothesis.title: Dispenser.claimOwnerIncentives Reverts When Reward and TopUp Are Both Zero
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In claimOwnerIncentives(), when both reward and topUp are zero, the code path skips the Treasury withdrawal (since reward + topUp == 0), leaving the `success` variable as its default value `false`. The function then hits `if (!success) { revert ClaimIncentivesFailed(...); }`, causing an uninformative revert instead of a graceful return with zero values.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a code logic defect. Any user calling claimOwnerIncentives when they have no accumulated incentives will receive a confusing ClaimIncentivesFailed error instead of a clean return. The `success` variable is only set inside the `if ((reward + topUp) > 0)` block, so when both are zero it remains false.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bool success; (default false) set inside conditional block; reverts when success is false even for legitimate zero-incentive case

preconditions: User calls claimOwnerIncentives with valid unitTypes and unitIds
The unit has no accumulated reward or topUp

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: success remains false when reward+topUp==0; line 460: if (!success) revert
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Legitimate call with zero incentives gets reverted with misleading error instead of returning (0, 0)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No special handling for zero-incentive case

analysis_questions: Can this cause issues for integration contracts that try-catch claim calls?
Does this affect off-chain tooling that checks for claim availability?
validation_requirements: Call claimOwnerIncentives for a unit with no incentives and verify the revert
Confirm the revert message is ClaimIncentivesFailed with zero values

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not directly exploitable for fund extraction. This is a code correctness issue causing confusing reverts for users with no accumulated incentives.
prerequisites: User calls claim with zero accumulated incentives

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: No direct financial impact, but breaks integrations expecting graceful zero returns
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users and integration contracts calling claimOwnerIncentives
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: N/A — user can check getOwnerIncentives first

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for zero-incentive claim path

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible in any zero-incentive claim attempt

validation_result.mechanism: uninitialized success flag causes revert on zero-incentive claim
validation_result.impact_summary: When both reward and topUp are zero, claimOwnerIncentives reverts with ClaimIncentivesFailed instead of returning gracefully, breaking integrations expecting clean zero returns.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies a code logic defect in Dispenser.claimOwnerIncentives. The variable `bool success` is declared at Dispenser.sol (approximately line 455) with a default value of `false`. It is only set to a non-false value inside the `if ((reward + topUp) > 0)` block. When Tokenomics.accountOwnerIncentives returns (0, 0) — which is a legitimate scenario for units with no accumulated incentives — the condition `(reward + topUp) > 0` evaluates to false, the entire block is skipped, and `success` remains `false`. The subsequent check `if (!success)` then triggers `revert ClaimIncentivesFailed(msg.sender, 0, 0)`. This is a code correctness defect: the function should either return (0, 0) gracefully or provide a distinct error message for the zero-incentive case. Note that accountOwnerIncentives in Tokenomics.sol still zeroes out the reward/topUp fields and sets lastEpoch to 0, meaning state changes have already been committed before the Dispenser reverts, but since the entire transaction reverts, these changes are rolled back. The practical impact is that any user or integration contract calling claimOwnerIncentives with valid unit types and IDs that happen to have zero accumulated incentives will receive an uninformative revert.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof of signal is accurate. Lines 439-462 of Dispenser.sol show: (1) `bool success;` declared with default false, (2) `success` only assigned inside the `if ((reward + topUp) > 0)` conditional, (3) `if (!success) { revert ClaimIncentivesFailed(...); }` executes unconditionally when both values are zero.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The `bool success` variable defaults to `false` and is only set inside the `if ((reward + topUp) > 0)` block. When both reward and topUp are zero (a legitimate scenario), the conditional block is skipped, `success` remains `false`, and the function hits `revert ClaimIncentivesFailed(msg.sender, 0, 0)` instead of returning (0, 0) gracefully.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls claimOwnerIncentives with valid unitTypes and unitIds
2. Reentrancy check passes, pause check passes
3. ITokenomics.accountOwnerIncentives returns (0, 0) because the units have no accumulated incentives
5. `if ((0 + 0) > 0)` evaluates to false — block is skipped entirely
7. `if (!false)` evaluates to true — enters revert branch
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The defect lies in the assumption that `(reward + topUp) == 0` is always an error condition. In reality, a user may legitimately own components/agents that have not yet accumulated any rewards or top-ups. The accountOwnerIncentives function in Tokenomics.sol (lines ~740-795) simply sums up the reward and topUp values from mapUnitIncentives, which can be zero if no donations have been directed to the user's services.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an attack scenario but a code correctness issue. Any user calling claimOwnerIncentives with units that have zero accumulated incentives will encounter a revert. This is especially problematic for integration contracts that batch-claim across multiple accounts or use try-catch patterns, as the misleading revert makes it difficult to distinguish between 'no incentives available' and an actual system failure. The scenario is highly realistic — any component or agent owner who has not yet received service donations or whose units have already been claimed will trigger this.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium severity code defect. No direct financial loss, but it breaks the expected interface contract: callers cannot distinguish 'zero incentives' from 'claim system failure'. Integration contracts using try-catch on claimOwnerIncentives will incorrectly classify zero-incentive cases as failures. Users must first call getOwnerIncentives (a view function) to check before claiming, adding friction and gas costs. The Tokenomics.accountOwnerIncentives function still performs state-modifying operations (zeroing balances, updating lastEpoch) before returning, but since the entire transaction reverts, these changes are harmlessly rolled back.
recommended_mitigations: Add an early return when both reward and topUp are zero: `if ((reward + topUp) == 0) { _locked = 1; return (0, 0); }`
Alternatively, initialize `bool success = true;` and only set it to false if the treasury withdrawal fails, keeping the revert only for actual withdrawal failures
Add a distinct error or require check: `if (reward == 0 && topUp == 0) { revert NoIncentivesToClaim(); }` with a clearer error name
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Tokenomics, Treasury, Dispenser, and mock registries. 2. Register a component via ComponentRegistry. 3. Do NOT donate to any service using that component. 4. Call Dispenser.claimOwnerIncentives with unitTypes=[0] and unitIds=[componentId] from the component owner. 5. Observe the revert with ClaimIncentivesFailed(owner, 0, 0).
uncertainties: Whether the development team considers this intentional behavior (i.e., claiming with zero incentives should always revert as a gas-saving signal) — however, the comment says 'Check if the claim is successful and has at least one non-zero incentive' which suggests they intended the zero case to be handled differently
additional_testing_needed: Test claimOwnerIncentives with units that have never received donations
Test claimOwnerIncentives after all incentives have been claimed (second claim attempt)
Test integration contract behavior when using try-catch around claimOwnerIncentives
